coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence intelligent sensing platform-integrated molecular imprinting for ultrasensitive visual detection of triclosan
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yu
Tang, Kangling
Zhou, Qin
Wang, Xiangni
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Triclosan (TCS) is an endocrine disruptor, which has been widely used in daily chemicals, resulting in the potential risk to the ecosystem and human health. Herein, a smartphone-integrated bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensing system was developed for ultrasensitive and intelligent visual microanalysis of TCS. Carbon dots (CDs) and bimetallic organic framework (MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2) were used as fluorescence sources to synthesize nanozyme fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer (MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP), which oxidized o-phenylenediamine to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), resulting in the derivation of a new fluorescence peak at 556 nm. In the existence of TCS, the fluorescence of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 at 450 nm was restored, the fluorescence of OPDox at 556 nm was suppressed, and the CDs fluorescence of at 686 nm remained constant. The color of triple-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor varied from yellow to pink to purple to blue. The response efficiency (F450/F556/F686) of this sensing platform based on the capillary waveguide effect demonstrated a significant linear relationship toward the concentration of TCS ranged from 1.0 × 10−12 to 1.5 × 10−10 M with the LOD of 8.0 × 10−13 M. Compared with dual-emission capillary fluorescence sensor, this sensing system has higher sensitivity and richer visual color. Combined with the smartphone-integrated portable sensing platform, the color of fluorescence was transformed into an RGB value to calculate TCS concentration with the LOD of 9.6 × 10−13 M, providing a novel method for intelligent visual microanalysis (18 μL/time) of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triple-emission capillary fluorescence
Bimetallic nanozyme
Intelligent visualization
Triclosan
Molecularly imprinted polymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence imprinted polymer
Fluorescence response mechanism and peroxidase-like activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2 and CDs at various ratios (the inset shows the fluorescence colors under 365 nm UV lamp).

(A) SEM image and (B) relevant elemental mapping of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2. (C) SEM image and (D) relevant elemental mapping of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NMIP. (E) TEM image of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NMIP. (F) Fe2p XPS spectrum and (G) Co2p XPS spectrum of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2. (H) Full XPS spectrum of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NMIP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NMIP + H2O2 + OPD system over reaction time. (B) Fluorescence spectra of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NMIP + H2O2 + OPD + TCS system. (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio of F450/F556/F686 and TCS concentration of 1.0 × 10−12- 1.5 × 10−10 M. (D) Fluorescence spectra of MOF-(Fe/Co)–NH2@CDs@NNIP + H2O2 + OPD + TCS system.

(A) Fluorescence colors captured by smartphone and corresponding RGB values. (B) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram after adding TCS. (C) Linear plots of the ratio of blue to red (B/R) toward various TCS concentrations. (D) Fluorescence response efficiency toward coexistent ions in the presence of TCS. (E) Fluorescence response efficiency toward analogues in the presence of TCS.
(A) Preparation process of triple-emission MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP for capillary fluorescence detection of TCS. (B) Schematic diagram of the peroxidase-like property of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP.
Determination results of TCS by fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor and HPLC.
Performance evaluation of several approaches for determining TCS.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence intelligent sensing platform-integrated molecular imprinting for ultrasensitive visual detection of triclosan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biological and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Triclosan (TCS) is an endocrine disruptor, which has been widely used in daily chemicals, resulting in the potential risk to the ecosystem and human health. Herein, a smartphone-integrated bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensing system was developed for ultrasensitive and intelligent visual microanalysis of TCS. Carbon dots (CDs) and bimetallic organic framework (MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2) were used as fluorescence sources to synthesize nanozyme fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer (MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP), which oxidized o-phenylenediamine to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), resulting in the derivation of a new fluorescence peak at 556 nm. In the existence of TCS, the fluorescence of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 at 450 nm was restored, the fluorescence of OPDox at 556 nm was suppressed, and the CDs fluorescence of at 686 nm remained constant. The color of triple-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor varied from yellow to pink to purple to blue. The response efficiency (F450/F556/F686) of this sensing platform based on the capillary waveguide effect demonstrated a significant linear relationship toward the concentration of TCS ranged from 1.0 × 10−12 to 1.5 × 10−10 M with the LOD of 8.0 × 10−13 M. Compared with dual-emission capillary fluorescence sensor, this sensing system has higher sensitivity and richer visual color. Combined with the smartphone-integrated portable sensing platform, the color of fluorescence was transformed into an RGB value to calculate TCS concentration with the LOD of 9.6 × 10−13 M, providing a novel method for intelligent visual microanalysis (18 μL/time) of environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Triclosan (TCS), a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, is present in many common household products including soap and toothpaste, which has been deemed as an endocrine disruptor. The extensive use of TCS resulted in the release of TCS into the sediment, wastewater and other water sources [1]. Studies showed the ecosystem and public health could be put at risk by TCS and its degradation products [2]. Currently, the main methods for detecting TCS included high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), electrochemical analysis (EA), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), et al [3–7] . However, these analytical methods demanded expensive equipment, longer detection periods and skilled worker [8]. A rapid, sensitive and intelligent visual analytical method for detection of TCS should be developed to protect the environment and public health.

Fluorescence capillary analysis (FCA) method employing a capillary tube instead of traditional fluorescence colorimetric plate, not only eliminats reagent waste, but also realizes the determination of trace sample (<18 μL/time) [9–11]. Our team has previously proposed a dual-emission capillary imprinted sensor for L-tyrosine analysis [12], which was not suitable for visual detection due to the narrow variation in the fluorescence color range of dual-emission. Interestingly, Wu et al. prepared a triple-emission fluorescence sensor with one-pot method for detection of chloramphenicol, which realized the abundant color change for visible analysis [13]. To our knowledge, triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor has not been reported.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of interesting fluorescence source owing to their high stability, good water solubility, and low cytotoxicity [14–16]. However, the aggregation of CDs induced fluorescence quenching, which hindered their application [17]. Some studies showed that encapsulating CDs into organic frameworks (CD@MOF) can lessen the aggregation of CDs, which can enhance their optical property [18]. Recently, the preparation of MOFs-based composites by encapsulating fluorescence guests in the cavities of MOFs has attracted high attentions [19–21]. It is worth mentioning that the MOFs with enzymatic properties have been developed for their high stability, multiple porous structures, low budget and good biocompatibility [22–24]. Specially, bimetallic MOFs constructed by adjusting the ratio of two metal ions to the organic ligand displayed more outstanding enzymatic catalytic activity due to the synergistic interaction between the two metals [25–27]. However, the low selectivity of MOFs-based enzyme has limited the advancement of MOFs-based fluorescence sensor [28]. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are a kind of man-made materials with high selectivity and specificity toward the template molecule [29]. Studies demonstrated the promising potential applications of integrating MOFs-based nanozymes with MIPs in biochemical research [30]. Certainly, a monometallic nanozyme dual-emission capillary imprinted sensor for triclocarban analysis has been proposed in our previous study [31], in which the enzymatic catalytic activity was insufficient.

Smartphones have become integral electronic products for our daily lives in the internet era. Recently, smartphone-assisted fluorescence detection has become a research hotspot [32,33]. The color recognition APP for smartphones has a powerful calculation function to quantify the colors captured by the camera and then converted to values representing the colors red, green and blue (RGB) [34]. As far as we know there are no reports on the development of a smartphone-integrated fluorescence sensor for the rapid and sensitive intelligent detection of TCS.

Herrin, a novel smartphone-integrated fluorescence capillary imprinted sensing platform was developed for ultrasensitive and intelligent visual detection of TCS (Scheme 1 A). The bimetallic nanozyme fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer (MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP) was prepared by using MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 (emission = 450 nm) and CDs (emission = 686 nm) as fluorescence sources. In the existence of OPD and H2O2, the peroxidase-like activity of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), resulting in the derivation of a new fluorescence peak at 556 nm (Scheme 1B). The bimetallic nanozyme fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was constructed successfully with triple-emission wavelengths at 450 nm, 556 nm, and 686 nm by directly inhaling MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP as well as OPD and H2O2 into the capillary. Combined with the APP image capture function of a portable smartphone, the color of bimetallic nanozyme fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor realized the rapid intelligent detection of TCS, which presents a novel approach for the ultrasensitive, selective, and intelligent detection of TCS.
Experimental section was covered in the Supplementary Material and the portable smartphone sensing platform was shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: A bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence imprinted polymer was prepared by one-pot method with MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 and CDs as fluorescence sources (Scheme 1A). Due to the peroxidase-like property of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, OPD was oxidized to OPDox with yellow fluorescence in the existence of H2O2. When the mixed solution of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP + H2O2 + OPD was inhaled into the capillary directly, a red-yellow-blue nanozyme fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was established with the triple-emission peaks at 450 nm, 556 nm, and 686 nm (Scheme 1B). Studies showed that the volume ratio of CDs to MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 affected the initial fluorescence color and the range of color changes of the fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence colors of the mixture solution changed insignificantly when 1.0 mL, 1.5 mL, 2.0 mL of CDs were added respectively into 1 mL of 1 mg·mL−1 MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 solution. Interestingly, 2.5 mL of CDs caused the color of the mixed solution to shift from dark blue to light blue. While the color shifted dark blue to light pink when 3.0 mL of CDs was added. Taking into considering the richer color change of the fluorescence sensor, 2.5 mL of CDs was chosen as the optimal volume. By taking advantage of the peroxidase-like property of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, a new peak at 556 nm was derived after the addition of H2O2 and OPD (Scheme 1B). The corresponding solution fluorescence color shifted from light blue to purple to pink to yellow. In addition, the results indicated no effect on the fluorescence response when only TCS or OPD was added into MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP solution (Fig. S2A and Fig. S2B), which further demonstrated the viability of development of triple-emission nanozyme fluorescence imprinted sensor.

The SEM technique was used to analysis the morphology of materials. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 displayed a spindle-like structures with a length of 1 µm and a width of 0.6 µm. Meanwhile, the element mappings of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 revealed that the distribution of C, N, O, Fe and Co in the substance was uniform (Fig. 2B). Interestingly, the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP exhibited a spherical shape shown in Fig. 2C and Si element in the element mappings was observed in Fig. 2D, which indicated that the successful formation of the imprinted layer. The MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NNIP showed the same shape (Fig. S3 A) of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP. The TEM image (Fig. 2E) further showed that the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 was effectively encapsulated by the imprinted layer. Compared with the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, the peaks intensity in the XRD patterns of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP decreased significantly shown in Fig. S3B, which was owed to the presence of an imprinted layer outside the material. The FT-IR of MOF-Fe-NH2 and MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 showed the same peaks at 556 cm−1, 1383 cm−1, 1578 cm−1 and 3468 cm−1, which were ascribed to the Fe-O bending vibration, the vibrations O-C=O of carboxyl group are asymmetrical and symmetrical, respectively. (Fig. S3C). In addition, the FT-IR of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 with diverse Fe/Co molar ratios were shown in Fig. S3D, in which the strength of the bending vibration of Fe-O reduced as the Co concentration increased, indicating the effective synthesis of bimetallic MOF with different proportions. Compared to the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, a series of new peaks at 481 cm−1, 776 cm−1, 1058 cm−1 were observed in the FT-IR of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP (Fig. S3E). These peaks were linked to Si-O vibration and Si-O-Si vibration, which proved the imprinted layer had been synthesized successfully. According to Fig. S3F, the full XPS spectrum of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2 showed that C, N, O, Fe, Co were distributed in the material, which proved the successful synthesis of bimetallic MOF. The binding energies of fine Fe spectrum at 725 eV, 711 eV, 729 eV, and 715 eV were attributed to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of Fe2+, Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of Fe3+, respectively (Fig. 2F) [31]. Additionally, the binding energies of fine Co spectrum at 794 eV, 784 eV were attributed to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 of Co2+, respectively [35]. The satellite peaks located at 803 eV and 788 eV were the fingerprints of Co2+ (Fig. 2G). Meanwhile, the XPS full spectrum of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP was analyzed. Compared to MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, Si 2p element was observed in Fig. 2H, which suggested that the imprinted layer had been prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response mechanism and peroxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence response mechanism and peroxidase-like activity of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP were covered in Fig. S4 and Supplementary Material. The results demonstrated that the IFE was responsible for the fluorescence quenching of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP. Additionally, compared with monometallic MOFs [25], the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP was prepared by varying the proportion of the two metal ions to the organic ligand, which displayed more excellent peroxidase-like activity due to the synergistic interaction between the two metals [26–28].

According to the optimized fluorescence response conditions, which shown in Fig. S5 and Supplementary Material, the analytical performance of smartphone-integrated triple-emission nanozyme fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor toward TCS was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of this sensing system over time within 10 min were shown in Fig. 3A. After the addition of TCS, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm was recovered with the increasing TCS concentration, while the fluorescence intensity at 556 nm was quenched and that at 686 nm remained constant (Fig. 3B). The fluorescence response efficiency of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP was expressed as the ratio of three fluorescence intensities ((F450/F556/F686)/(F450/F556/F686)0). The corresponding linear equation was Y = 0.0116 X + 1.0223 (R2 = 0.998) with TCS concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−12 ∼ 1.5 × 10−10 M (Fig. 3C). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 8.0 × 10−13 M based on (3S/N). The higher fluorescence sensitivity was attributed to the waveguide effect of the ring resonator, which amplifies the fluorescence signal [11,12]. However, the generated peak at 556 nm of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NNIP was weak (Fig. 3D). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm, 556 nm, and 686 nm of the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NNIP were not changed when TCS was added, which was ascribed to the lack of imprinted cavities in the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NNIP hindered the entry of H2O2 to react with MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2, resulting in extremely weak peroxidase activity. In addition, the fluorescence response efficiency of the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP was also assessed using a traditional fluorescence detection device. As shown in Fig. S6, the results showed a good linearity between (F450/F556/F686)/(F450/F556/F686)0 and the concentration of TCS varied from 1.5 × 10−10 to 4.0 × 10−8 M. The corresponding linear equation was Y = 0.0015 X + 1.008 (R2 = 0.998) with the LOD of 7.8 × 10−9 M. The results indicated that the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP capillary fluorescence sensor has superior performance for the detection of TCS. Additionally, the solution colors captured by the smartphone under different TCS concentrations were shown in Fig. 4 A. The results showed that the color of fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor changes from yellow to pink to purple to light blue as the concentration of TCS vary from 1.0 × 10−12 to 1.5 × 10−10 M. The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram in Fig. 4B was consistent with fluorescence color variation of the sensor. As illustration in Fig. 4C, the blue and red channels (B/R) demonstrated a significant linear relationship with the concentration of TCS, and the linear equation was Y = 0.0139X + 0.5746 (R2 = 0.994) with the limit of LOD of 9.6 × 10−13 M (Based on 3S/N). Thus, a portable fluorescence sensing platform integrated with smartphone was constructed for fast and intelligent detection TCS without expensive instrument.

To verify the selectivity of smartphone-integrated capillary fluorescence imprinted sensor, the fluorescence response performance of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP toward some potential interfering ions and analogues were discussed. The chemical structures of analogues were exhibited in Fig. S7. As shown in Fig. 4D and Fig. 4E, when TCS was added, the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP had a significant fluorescence response. In contrast, when various interfering ions or analogues were added alone, the fluorescence response of the sensor was negligible. While TCS and various interfering ions or analogues were added together, the fluorescence response was similar with that of TCS alone, which demonstrated that the bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor has ultraselective toward TCS. Additionally, the capillary fluorescence imprinted sensor remained constant fluorescence response performance over 30 days (Fig. S8A), indicating that it had good stability. By monitoring the intensity of the fluorescence throughout five remove-rebind cycles, the reproducibility of MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP sensor was examined. Interestingly, the RSD of 5 replicates was 3.8% (Fig. S8B), demonstrating the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP had good reproducibility.

The recoveries of the actual samples were examined by the standard addition method to confirm the application of bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor. As indicated in Table1 , the recoveries of TCS measured with the fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor were 97.60 % −102.78 %, which were similar with the results of high-performance liquid chromatography (The test conditions were shown in the Supplementary Material). Furthermore, the performance of fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor with other detection methods for detection TCS was compared. As listed in Table 2 , compared with other detection methods, the MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2@CDs@NMIP sensor showed a lower detection limit and wider detection range, which can achieve micro-volume (18 µL/time) and intelligent visual detection.

In summary, a smartphone-integrated bimetallic nanozyme triple-emission fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor was developed for ultrasensitive, intelligent visual detection of TCS successfully by optimizing the ratio of CDs and MOF-(Fe/Co)-NH2. The fluorescence colors captured by the smartphone were converted to RGB values to establish a linear relationship between B/R values and TCS concentration without the expensive instrument. The fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor has good linearity in TCS concentration range of 1.0 × 10−12 − 1.5 × 10−10 M with a LOD of 8.0 × 10−13 M. Compared with dual-emission capillary fluorescence sensor, the smartphone-integrated triple-emission bimetallic nanozyme fluorescence capillary imprinted sensor realized ultrasensitive microanalysis (18 µL/time) of TCS with richer color changes from yellow to pink to purple to light blue. This offered a novel approach for the highly sensitive and selective intelligent visual detection of TCS.

Yu Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Kangling Tang: Resources, Methodology. Qin Zhou: Data curation. Xiangni Wang: Formal analysis. Zhaohui Zhang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
